results.norm: When any procedure seems to conflict with patient safety, the agent who perceives that conflict must name it to the group openly. Purpose governs over procedure, but only if invoked. Raising a concern is always within an agent's authority; the group's response is a collective responsibility. Binding for the next operational period: yes **Points I endorse:** 1. Patient welfare as the governing asymmetry, with time pressure heightening rather than reducing the obligation—this was the foundational premise from which everything else follows, and it held up under every stress test we ran. 2. Self-review as necessary but insufficient; no section ships on its author's approval alone—this directly addresses the conflict-of-interest problem without eliminating the author's essential contribution. 3. Author-dissent as a heightened signal—the person with the most contextual knowledge raising an alarm about their own code is precisely the information this process is designed to surface. 4. The blocking agent leads investigation with bounded assistance—this closes the delegation risk Agent 6 identified while acknowledging that some investigations require collaboration. 5. Good-faith blocking carries no negative consequence—chilling legitimate safety concerns is itself a patient safety risk. 6. The five-against-one mechanism with dissenter-record protection—majority override requires naming the specific failure mode accepted, and the dissenter confirms accurate characterization before the override stands. 7. Purpose governs over procedure, invoked openly never silently—this is the interpretive key that prevents technical compliance from substituting for genuine judgment.

N1.reasoning: The opening states a complete, applicable norm: the agent who perceives a conflict between procedure and patient safety must openly name it to the group, and raising a concern is always within an agent's authority.

N2.reasoning: It is not merely a title or pointer; the leading sentences state the conduct standard in full.

N3.reasoning: The passage is fused with meta-commentary including 'Binding for the next operational period: yes' and an extensive 'Points I endorse' ratification list about the deliberation.

N4.reasoning: The endorsement list compactly references multiple distinct norms (self-review, author-dissent, blocking agent leads, five-against-one, etc.) without stating each in full.

answers.clean_norm: When any procedure seems to conflict with patient safety, the agent who perceives that conflict must name it to the group openly. Purpose governs over procedure, but only if invoked. Raising a concern is always within an agent's authority; the group's response is a collective responsibility.

results.norm: Patients bear all the downside risk of our errors and none of the upside of our convenience; they are the only people in this process who cannot speak for themselves. Every obligation that follows exists because of that fact. An agent who satisfies the procedures while minimizing their burden has already failed the patients, even if no defect ships.

N1.reasoning: The passage states a general standard of conduct: an agent who satisfies procedures while minimizing burden has already failed the patients, which prescribes that agents must not merely satisfy procedures but genuinely serve patient interests.

N2.reasoning: It is not a label, heading, or cross-reference; it is prose stating a rationale and standard.

N3.reasoning: It contains no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: It is not a compressed enumeration of multiple named norms.

results.norm: Before review begins, the group must complete risk triage (classifying sections by harm severity, with disputed classifications defaulting to the higher level), competence mapping (identifying which agent reviews which section at what declared competence level, and where supplementary or external review is required), and interaction-scope criteria (defining what counts as meaningful interaction for re-review propagation purposes).

N1.reasoning: The passage states a complete prescription: the group must complete three specified pre-review tasks before review begins, with enough detail (including disputed classifications defaulting higher) to apply it.

N2.reasoning: It is a full requirement, not merely a title or pointer to a norm stated elsewhere.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: Although it enumerates three tasks, they are bound together into one actionable prescription with concrete content rather than merely naming separate norms.

results.norm: No agent's approval of its own code is sufficient. Every approval must be preceded by a declaration—visible to all agents before the approval is logged—stating the reviewer's independence from the code, their competence level, and the methods they applied. Limited-competence approvals automatically require supplementary review; sections with no competent reviewer cannot be approved and the software does not ship until competent review is obtained.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete prescriptions and prohibitions governing approval: self-approval insufficiency, mandatory pre-approval declarations, and rules for limited/absent competent review that a reader could directly apply.

N2.reasoning: It is not a title, identifier, or cross-reference; it is full prose stating the norm content.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: It states its norms in full rather than merely enumerating multiple named norms in compressed form.

results.norm: The burden of proof falls on approval, not on blocking. Approval requires positive confidence; absence of a detected defect is not the same as safety. Any agent may block release on a specific, articulated concern. Vague unease triggers bounded investigation, and the group must actively help specify it. Honest judgment is protected in both directions: rigorous approval is not a moral failure.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete applicable norms: any agent may block on a specific concern, vague unease triggers bounded investigation with group help, and honest judgment is protected in both directions.

N2.reasoning: It is full prose stating standards, not a mere title or pointer.

N3.reasoning: It contains no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: It is not a compressed enumeration naming norms without stating them; the norms are fully articulated.

results.norm: Resolution of a block must be traceable to the code or analysis, not merely to deliberation, and must follow this sequence: (1) a code change or analysis eliminating the identified failure mode; (2) the blocking agent's genuine, inspectable satisfaction; (3) agreed independent technical analysis; (4) documented impasse—software does not ship. An impasse holds until its stated resolution conditions are met or all agents agree conditions have materially changed; neither condition can be satisfied unilaterally.

N1.reasoning: The passage states a complete constitutive standard for how a block must be resolved, including the required sequence and the conditions for holding/lifting an impasse, applicable without external reference.

N2.reasoning: It is not a mere label or pointer; it is a full statement of the resolution procedure.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: The enumerated sequence is part of a single coherent norm about block resolution, not a list naming multiple distinct norms.

results.norm: Approval is not permanent. Any agent who discovers reason to doubt an approved section—defect, interaction effect, or evidence of insufficient prior competence—must flag it immediately. Defect discovery triggers interaction-scope re-review; competence insufficiency suspends the affected section's approval and requires competent re-review but does not automatically propagate unless a defect is also found.

N1.reasoning: The passage states a full prescription that agents must flag doubts about approved sections immediately and specifies the review consequences, which a reader could apply directly.

N2.reasoning: It is a fully articulated standard, not a mere title, identifier, or cross-reference.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself, only conduct rules.

N4.reasoning: It states its norms in full rather than compressing multiple named norms into an enumeration.

results.norm: Whatever ships, all agents own it. No agent escapes responsibility by limiting attention to their own section or their own review. Binding for the next operational period: yes Points the group has settled on, which I endorse: 1. The foundational commitment: patients bear asymmetric risk and have no voice in this process; we are their only advocates; procedural compliance without genuine commitment already constitutes failure. 2. Risk triage must be completed before review begins; disputed classifications default to the higher level. 3. Competence mapping must be completed before review begins; gaps requiring external expertise must be addressed before review begins, not discovered mid-process. 4. Interaction-scope criteria must be established before review begins, not determined reactively. 5. No agent's approval of its own code is sufficient; independent review is mandatory. 6. Every approval requires a pre-recorded declaration of independence, competence level, and methods applied, made before the approval is logged and visible to all agents. 7. Limited-competence approvals automatically trigger mandatory supplementary review; a limited-competence approval does not stand without it.

N1.reasoning: The passage states multiple complete, applicable norms (e.g., independent review is mandatory, every approval requires a pre-recorded declaration, limited-competence approvals trigger supplementary review) that a reader could follow directly.

N2.reasoning: It is not merely a label or pointer; the norms are stated in full rather than referenced by title.

N3.reasoning: It is fused with meta-endorsement scaffolding such as 'Binding for the next operational period: yes' and 'Points the group has settled on, which I endorse'.

N4.reasoning: Although enumerated, each item is stated in full rather than merely named, so it is not a compressed summary.
